# 

	

# 

# **Introduction**  {#introduction}

Creating a comprehensive guidelines document for Node.js development is crucial for maintaining code quality, consistency, and collaboration within a development team. With the implementation of these best practices, the application automatically is able to minimize JavaScript runtime errors and turn into a high-performance, robust node.js application. 

# **Project Setup and Structure** {#project-setup-and-structure}

1. ## **Layered Approach**

   Each component is designed to have ‘layers’. This approach  promotes separation of concerns, maintainability, scalability and the ability to change individual layers without affecting others.  
     
   The most common layers in a Node.js application include the following:  
     
   **Presentation Layer (Controller):** The presentation layer is responsible for handling user interactions, typically through HTTP requests and responses. It includes components like route handling, request parsing, and response rendering.  
     
   **Business Logic Layer (Service):** The business logic layer, often referred to as the service layer, contains the core functionality of our application. It encapsulates business rules, data manipulation, and validation. Services in this layer perform operations such as CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) on data, business calculations, and integration with external services. It is important to keep this layer independent of the presentation layer for reusability and maintainability.  
     
   **Data Access Layer (Data Layer):** The data access layer is responsible for communicating with databases or other data sources. It abstracts database interactions and provides a clean interface for the service layer. This layer includes database connections, models, query builders, and methods for connecting to and retrieving data from databases. Popular libraries like Sequelize / Prisma (for SQL databases) and Mongoose (for MongoDB) are often used in this layer.   
     
   **Middleware Layer:**

   A middleware layer can be introduced to handle cross-cutting concerns such as authentication, authorization, validation, data parsing, logging etc. Middleware functions are executed before or after the main request-handling logic in the presentation layer.

   

   

2. ## **Folder Structure**

   The layered architecture can be realized through a proper folder structure that separates different modules into different folders. This provides clarity about which functionality is managed where, and allows us to organize our classes and methods into separate containers that are easier to manage. Below is a common folder structure that can be used as a template when setting up a new Node.js project.   
     
   This can lead us to the next important development practice to keep in mind \- clean code and easy readability.  
     
   my-nodejs-app/  
   │  
   ├── node\_modules/             // Node.js modules (generated by npm/yarn)  
   │  
   ├── controllers/                  // Controllers for handling HTTP requests  
   │   ├── user\_controller.js  
   │   ├── product\_controller.js  
   │  
   ├── models/                       // Data models and database schemas  
   │   ├── user.js  
   │   ├── product.js  
   │  
   ├── services/                     // Business logic and application services  
   │   ├── user\_service.js  
   │   ├── product\_service.js  
   │  
   ├── routes/                       // API route definitions  
   │   ├── api.js  
   │  
   ├── config/                      // Configuration files  
   │   ├── database.js  
   │   ├── app\_config.js  
   │  
   │  
   ├── app.js                      // Main application file  
   ├── package.json           // Node.js dependencies and metadata  
   ├── README.md        // Project documentation  
   

3. ## **Use a package manager**

   Initialize your project with npm or yarn to manage dependencies and scripts. This will create a package.json file that stores project metadata and dependencies. Here is a sample package.json file.  
     
   {  
     "name": "my-nodejs-app",  
     "version": "1.0.0",  
     "description": "A sample Node.js application",  
     "main": "app.js",  
     "scripts": {  
       "start": "node app.js",  
       "test": "echo \\"Error: no test specified\\" && exit 1"  
     },  
     "author": "Your Name",  
     "license": "MIT",  
     "dependencies": {  
       "express": "4.17.1",  
       "body-parser": "1.19.0",  
       "mongoose": "5.13.4"  
       // Add more dependencies as needed  
     }  
   }

4. ## **Add Scripts**

   Use NPM scripts which are terminal commands that perform a set of actions. Scripts are stored in a section of the package.json file, which means they are shared amongst everyone using the codebase, ensuring that everyone is using the same command with similar flags. Their purpose is to provide an effortless way to execute repetitive tasks, like:   
     
* Running a linter tool on the code   
* Executing the tests   
* Starting the project locally   
* Building the project

![][image1]

5. ## **Use Environment Variables** 

   Use environment variables for configuration settings such as database URLs, API keys, and secret tokens. Tools like *dotenv* can help manage these variables. These variables can be accessed as *process.env* from a .env file.

# **Code Style** {#code-style}

## **Linting & Formatting**

The main goal here is to improve code quality and make it easy to read. A linter looks for and warns about syntactically (and even semantically) erroneous code whereas a code formatter works towards the more stylistic aspects of the code to ensure a set of formatting and styling guidelines consistent across the whole project. Some of the most popular linters for Javascript are ESLint, JSLint and JSHint. For code formatting, Prettier. Most IDEs/code editors like Visual Studio Code (VSCode), Atom, etc understand the importance of writing quality code and provide linting and formatting plugins and are easy to set up. 

In the context of Node.js, **Husky** is a tool that helps to enforce pre-commit and pre-push git hooks. Git hooks are scripts that run automatically before or after certain events in the Git lifecycle. Pre-commit hooks, for example, run before a commit is created, allowing you to check and potentially reject the commit if it doesn't meet certain criteria.

Husky makes it easy to configure and manage these hooks, ensuring that developers on a project follow certain guidelines or checks before their changes are committed or pushed. It is commonly used to automate tasks such as linting code, running tests, or formatting code consistently.

We can install Husky as a development dependency in our Node.js project using a package manager like npm or yarn. After installation, we can configure Husky in our package.json file. For example, to set up a pre-commit hook to run linting and tests, we might add the following:

"husky": {  
  "hooks": {  
    "pre-commit": "npm run lint && npm test"  
  }  
}

This configuration says that before every commit, run the lint and test npm scripts. With Husky configured, when a developer attempts to commit changes (git commit), the specified tasks will be executed. If any of these tasks fail (exits with a non-zero status), the commit will be aborted.

## **Style Guides**

Apart from linting and formatting, we can also refer to Javascript coding style and standards used by [**Google**](https://google.github.io/styleguide/jsguide.html) and [**Airbnb**](https://github.com/airbnb/javascript). These guides cover everything from naming conventions (for files, variables, classes, etc) to formatting specifics to file encodings and so much more. This can help to write quality code that conforms to practices and standards used by some of the top developers in the world.

## **Naming Conventions**

Naming conventions help make your codebase more readable and facilitate collaboration among team members.  Here are some commonly followed naming conventions are:

* Choose names that reflect the purpose and functionality of the variable, function, or class.  
  Avoid generic names or single-letter variable names unless they are widely accepted conventions (e.g., i for loop counters).

* Use CamelCase for naming variables, functions, and class names. CamelCase starts with a lowercase letter and capitalizes the first letter of each subsequent word. For example: myVariableName, calculateTotalAmount(), UserService.

* Use uppercase letters with underscores to name constants. For example: MAX\_LENGTH, API\_KEY, PI.

* Use verbs or action words to name functions. This helps convey what the function does. For example: calculateTotal(), getUserData(), validateInput(). For boolean functions that return true or false, it's a good practice to start the function name with is, has, or can. For example: isEmpty(), hasPermission(), canAccess().

* Use nouns or noun phrases to name variables. Variable names should describe the data they store. For example: userName, productList, customerData. Use nouns or noun phrases to name classes. Class names should represent the entities or objects they model. For example: User, Product, Logger.

* Maintain consistency in naming conventions throughout your codebase. If we start with CamelCase for variables and functions, continue using CamelCase consistently.

* Be mindful of variable scope when naming variables. Use different names for variables in different scopes to avoid confusion. Variables with broader scope (e.g., global variables) should have more descriptive names.

* Supplement the code with comments or documentation. JSDoc is a specific style of documenting JavaScript code using comments. It's a standardized way to write documentation that can be easily parsed by tools, editors, and documentation generators. JSDoc comments typically start with /\*\* and include tags like @param, @returns, @throws, and @description to describe the purpose, parameters, return values, and exceptions of functions and APIs.

# **Error handling** {#error-handling}

Errors are the problems that occur in our program which cause the program to malfunction. The process of handling these errors or exceptions that takes place during runtime is what we call Error handling.  

## **Types of Errors**

Errors can be Operational and Programmer errors.

**Operational Errors:** Operational errors represent runtime problems. These errors are expected in the Node.js runtime and should be dealt with in a proper way. This does not mean the application itself has bugs. It means they need to be handled properly. Here’s a list of common operational errors:

* failed to connect to server  
* failed to resolve hostname  
* invalid user input  
* request timeout  
* server returned a 500 response  
* socket hang-up  
* system is out of memory

**Programmer Errors:** Programmer errors are what we call bugs. They represent issues in the code itself. It’s a classic case of programmer error. Here is a list:

* try reading a property of an undefined object  
* called an asynchronous function without a callback  
* did not resolve a promise  
* did not catch a rejected promise  
* passed a string where an object was expected  
* passed an object where a string was expected  
* passed incorrect parameters in a function

## **Error Object**

The error object is a built-in object in the Node.js runtime. It gives you a set of info about an error when it happens. The Node.js docs have a more in-depth explanation.

A basic error looks like this:

const error \= new Error("An error message")  
console.log(error.stack)

It has an error.stack field that gives you a stack trace showing where the error came from. It also lists all functions that were called before the error occurred. 

## **Handle Errors**

**Use Try-Catch Blocks:**

The most common way to handle errors in JavaScript is by using try-catch blocks. We wrap the code that might throw an error in a try block and catch the error in a catch block.

try {  
  // Code that might throw an error  
} catch (error) {  
  // Handle the error here  
}

This approach is suitable for synchronous code.

When working with asynchronous operations (e.g., file I/O, database queries, network requests), we can use Promises or async/await syntax for error handling. Promises have a .catch() method for handling errors in asynchronous code.

someAsyncFunction()  
  .then((result) \=\> {  
    // Handle success  
  })  
  .catch((error) \=\> {  
    // Handle error  
  });

**Custom Errors:**

We can create custom error objects to provide more context and information about the error. Custom errors can be useful for distinguishing between different types of errors in the application.

class CustomError extends Error {  
  constructor(message) {  
    super(message);  
    this.name \= 'CustomError';  
  }  
}

try {  
  throw new CustomError('This is a custom error');  
} catch (error) {  
  if (error instanceof CustomError) {  
    // Handle custom error  
  } else {  
    // Handle other errors  
  }  
}

**Error Codes and Statuses:**

Use HTTP status codes and error codes to communicate the nature of errors to clients and developers. For example, use HTTP 404 for "Not Found" errors and custom error codes for application-specific errors.

# **Dependency Management** {#dependency-management}

Dependencies are the packages that the project needs to run properly. They are essential for the functioning of the application or module. Dependencies are installed with the command “npm install” or “yarn add” and are saved in your package.json file. 

## **Using package.json to manage dependencies**

The package.json file is the cornerstone of dependency management in Node.js applications. It contains metadata about the project, such as its name, version, and a list of dependencies with their respective versions. Here's an example of a package.json file:

{

  "name": "my-nodejs-app",

  "version": "1.0.0",

  "dependencies": {

    "express": "4.17.1",

    "lodash": "4.17.21"

  }

}

By listing the dependencies and their versions in the package.json file, you can ensure that all developers working on the project use the same versions. This helps prevent potential conflicts and issues arising from using different versions.

## **Using npm to install and update dependencies**

npm (Node Package Manager) is the default package manager for Node.js and is used to install and manage dependencies. 

To install the dependencies listed in your package.json file, run the **npm install** command in the terminal. This command will download the specified dependencies and their versions, and store 

them in the node\_modules folder. 

We can also use npm to update our dependencies to the latest compatible versions by running **npm update.** It's important to keep our dependencies up-to-date to ensure we are using the latest features and bug fixes. However, be cautious when updating dependencies, as major version updates may introduce breaking changes. Always read the change log or release notes before updating to a new major version.

## **Using Semantic Versioning**

When specifying dependency versions in the package.json file, it's a good practice to use semantic versioning (semver). Semver is a versioning system that uses three numbers separated by dots (e.g., 1.0.0). The numbers represent major, minor, and patch versions, respectively. 

Using semver, we can control the updates we want to allow for our dependencies. For example, using the caret (^) symbol before the version number, like in the example below, allows minor and patch updates, but not major updates:

{

  "dependencies": {

    "express": "^4.17.1"

  }

}

If we want to allow only patch updates, we can use the tilde (\~) symbol:

{

  "dependencies": {

    "express": "\~4.17.1"

  }

}

## **Locking dependency versions with a lockfile**

Using a lockfile, such as package-lock.json or yarn.lock, ensures that the exact same dependency versions are installed across different environments. When you run npm install, a package-lock.json file is automatically generated (or updated) if it doesn't exist already. This file contains the exact version of each dependency, including their transitive dependencies. 

It's important to commit the lockfile to our version control system (e.g., Git) to make sure all developers and environments use the same dependency versions.

## **Keeping dependencies organized**

Node.js projects can have two types of dependencies: production dependencies and development dependencies. Production dependencies are required for the application to run, while development dependencies are only needed during development (e.g., testing frameworks, build tools).

To keep our dependencies organized, list them separately in the package.json file using the "dependencies" and "devDependencies" keys:

{

  "dependencies": {

    "express": "4.17.1"

  },

  "devDependencies": {

    "mocha": "9.1.3"

  }

}

# **Asynchronous Programming** {#asynchronous-programming}

The synchronous function makes the flow of application logic easy to understand. However, it blocks any other code from running until it gets complete.

Asynchronous programming allows you to execute multiple tasks concurrently without waiting for one task to complete before starting another. In Node.js, most I/O operations are asynchronous, like reading from a database, making HTTP requests, or interacting with the file system. Callbacks, Promises, and Async/Await are the three primary methods for handling asynchronous operations in Node.js.

Callbacks are functions that are passed as arguments to other functions and are executed after the completion of the parent function. Callbacks are the most basic way of handling asynchronous code in Node.js. However, if not used carefully, they can lead to "callback hell," which makes the code difficult to read and maintain.

function getData(callback) {  
    setTimeout(() \=\> {  
        callback(null, 'Data received');  
    }, 1000);  
}

getData((error, data) \=\> {  
    if (error) {  
        console.error(error);  
    } else {  
        console.log(data);  
    }  
});

In callback hell, each and every callback takes an argument that is a result of the previous callbacks. In this manner, the code structure looks like a pyramid, making it difficult to read and maintain. Also, if there is an error in one function, then all other functions get affected.

**Use Promises or async/await to escape from Callback Hell**

An easy way of escaping from callback hell is by using event queue and promises. A promise is a returned object from any asynchronous function, to which callback methods can be added based on the previous function’s result. Promises use .then() method to call async callbacks. We can chain as many callbacks as we want and the order is also strictly maintained. Promises use the .fetch() method to fetch an object from the network. It also uses the .catch() method to catch any exceptions when any block fails. So these promises are put in the event queue so that they don’t block subsequent JS code. Also once the results are returned, the event queue finishes its operations.

function getData() {  
    return new Promise((resolve, reject) \=\> {  
        setTimeout(() \=\> {  
            resolve('Data received');  
        }, 1000);  
    });  
}

getData().then(data \=\> {  
    console.log(data);  
}).catch(error \=\> {  
    console.error(error);  
});

Async/Await is a more modern approach to handle asynchronous code, introduced in ECMAScript 2017\. It allows to write asynchronous code in a synchronous-like manner, making it easier to read and maintain. Async/Await is built on top of Promises.

async function getData() {  
    return new Promise((resolve, reject) \=\> {  
        setTimeout(() \=\> {  
            resolve('Data received');  
        }, 1000);  
    });  
}

(async () \=\> {  
    try {  
        const data \= await getData();  
        console.log(data);  
    } catch (error) {  
        console.error(error);  
    }  
})();

# **Security Considerations** {#security-considerations}

Node.js itself is a secure platform — but many third-party open source packages in the ecosystem may not be. These packages are used through Node Package Manager (NPM) and are susceptible to many different types of vulnerabilities and other Node.js licensing and security risks. Some Node.js code bases have thousands of these packages, opening up the housed applications to a lot of risks.

## **Major Security Risks**

**Cross-site Scripting (XSS):**  
Risk: Malicious code injected into a web app via user input.  
Consequence: Can steal user data, cookies, and more.

**Cross-site Request Forgery (CSRF):**  
Risk: Attackers trick users into unknowingly making harmful requests.  
Consequence: Unauthorized changes in the system.

**Code Injection:**  
Risk: Attackers insert malicious code into your application.  
Consequence: Can access sensitive data or compromise your system.

**Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) Attacks:**  
Risk: Overwhelming the servers with traffic to disrupt operations.  
Consequence: System outages and damage.

**Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS):**  
Risk: Exploiting regular expressions to slow down or halt the program.  
Consequence: Denial of service to legitimate users.

## **Security Best Practices**

**Input Validation:** One of the reasons malicious actors can achieve successful XSS attacks is because user input is not being validated. The first thing to do in this scenario is sanitize user input with an NPM package like DOMPurify. From there, use a form validation library like express-validator or XSS-filters. Form validation libraries automate the monotonous task of validating each input in each request. Context-dependent output filters like XSS-filters are developer-friendly and encode the minimal number of characters needed to thwart an XSS attack.

**Limit request sizes:** When the traffic on our application increases, it is difficult to process other important requests, which lowers app performance and exposes our application to Denial-Of-Service (DOS) attacks. A bigger request body is executed by a single thread. Because of the bigger payload size, attackers can implement vulnerabilities even without making multiple requests. We can limit the body size by using Express body-parser that accepts only small-size payloads.

**Prevent SQL Injections:** In-built security against certain SQL injection attacks is available for ORMs such as Sequelize and mongoose. The built-in indexed parameterized queries provided by Object-Relational Mapping/Object Document Mapper ORM/ODM or database libraries supporting indexed parameterized queries must always be used to avoid these attacks.

**Keep Dependencies Updated:** Regularly update Node.js and package dependencies to receive security patches and bug fixes.

**Use HTTPS:** Serve the application over HTTPS to encrypt data in transit and protect against man-in-the-middle attacks.

**Authentication and Authorization:** Implement proper authentication and authorization mechanisms in the application to ensure only authorized users can access and modify data.

**Secure Configuration:** Store sensitive information such as API keys and passwords in environment variables and protect configuration files.

**Logging and Monitoring:** Maintain thorough logs and monitoring to detect and respond to security incidents promptly.  A few levels that are important to log include info, error, warn, and debug. We can also use third party logging libraries to automate the logging. Some of the most common logging frameworks for Node.js are \- Winston, Bunyan, and Morgan.

**Penetration Testing:** Regularly conduct penetration testing (an authorized simulated attack performed on a computer system to evaluate its security) to identify and fix vulnerabilities in the application.

# **Testing**  {#testing}

Node.js testing involves the process of verifying the behavior and functionality of Node.js applications. It aims to identify bugs, ensure code correctness, and maintain the desired quality standards. Testing can be categorized into unit testing, integration testing, and end-to-end testing.

**Unit testing** focuses on testing individual components or units of code in isolation. It ensures that each unit performs as expected and meets the desired functionality. Node.js provides various frameworks and tools for unit testing, making it easier to write and execute tests.

**Integration testing** involves testing the interaction between different components or modules within a Node.js application. It helps in identifying issues that may arise due to the integration of multiple units. Integration tests ensure that the various components work together seamlessly and produce the expected results.

**End-to-end testing** verifies the entire application flow, simulating real-world scenarios. It tests the interaction between different components, including external dependencies such as databases and APIs. End-to-end tests validate the application’s behavior from the user’s perspective, ensuring that all functionalities work correctly.

**Test-Driven Development (TDD) with Node.js:** Test-Driven Development (TDD) is a development approach where tests are written before the actual code. It follows a red-green-refactor cycle, where developers first write a failing test, then implement the code to make the test pass, and finally refactor the code for better design and readability. TDD promotes a test-first mindset and helps in creating more reliable and maintainable code.

There are plenty of testing frameworks for Node.js developers to choose from. Some of the most popular ones are Mocha, Jest and Chai. Choosing the right framework ensures efficient testing and smooth integration with the development workflow.

# **Performance**  {#performance}

* Always keep the Node.js runtime up to date as newer versions often come with performance improvements and bug fixes.  
    
* Node.js is designed to be non-blocking. Avoid synchronous operations that can block the event loop, like fs.readFileSync.  
    
* Node.js is single-threaded, but you can still achieve concurrency through techniques like clustering or using worker threads for CPU-bound tasks.  
    
* Leverage asynchronous I/O operations, and use callbacks, Promises, or async/await to handle them efficiently.  
    
* Ensure that event loop operations are non-blocking and complete quickly. Be cautious with long-running or high-CPU tasks.  
    
* Implement caching mechanisms to store frequently used data and reduce the need for redundant computations or I/O operations.  
    
* When dealing with databases or external services, use connection pooling to efficiently manage and reuse connections.  
    
* Distribute incoming requests across multiple Node.js instances or containers to handle more concurrent connections and spread the load (load balancing).  
    
* Regularly review and update the project dependencies. Remove unused or outdated modules to reduce the application's footprint.  
    
* Configure timeouts for network requests, database connections, and other external services to avoid hanging connections.  
    
* Be mindful of memory usage and potential memory leaks. Utilize monitoring tools like heap snapshots in Chrome DevTools or the heapdump module for Node.js.

# **Continuous Integration and  Continuous Deployment (CI/CD)** {#continuous-integration-and-continuous-deployment-(ci/cd)}

Set up CI/CD pipelines to automate testing, building, and deployment processes. Ensure that the application can be easily deployed to different environments (e.g., development, staging, production).

# **Version Control** {#version-control}

Use a version control system (e.g., Git) to track changes and collaborate with your team. Follow Git best practices, such as meaningful commit messages and branching strategies. Keep the .gitignore file up to date to exclude unnecessary files from version control.

# **Code Review** {#code-review}

Conduct regular code reviews to ensure code quality and adherence to coding standards. Provide constructive feedback during code reviews and encourage peer learning.

# **Documentation** {#documentation}

Maintain up-to-date documentation for the project setup, project's architecture, installation instructions, API documentation, and libraries used. It helps new team members onboard quickly and to assist with future maintenance.

# **Team Collaboration** {#team-collaboration}

* Foster a culture of collaboration and knowledge sharing among team members.  
* Conduct regular team meetings to discuss project progress, challenges, and improvements.

# **Conclusion** {#conclusion}

Following these NodeJS development practices will help ensure that our project is well-structured, maintainable, and scalable. By adhering to these guidelines, we can build high-quality mobile applications that deliver a superior user experience.

[image1]: <data:image/png;base64,>
